verdict.reason: Delpero's period piece offers beautiful mountain cinematography but moves at a contemplative pace that may test patience. The film's quiet observations reward viewers seeking unhurried storytelling.

ratings.plot: The story of a hitman for the drug cartels in Ciudad Juarez, Mexico.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most chilling documentaries ever made about cartel violence. Rosi's decision to keep his subject anonymous and shoot entirely in a motel room creates an intimacy that makes the horror feel immediate.

ratings.plot: A New York woman apprentices for a dance company and throws herself headlong into her dreams, even as the possibility of realizing them dwindles.

verdict.reason: Greta Gerwig is magnetic but this plays just as well streaming. The black and white photography looks nice theatrically but the intimate scale suits smaller screens.

ratings.plot: An Icelandic writer preserves vanishing glaciers, departed grandparents, family memories, and flowing time in his personal archives, transforming loss into a time capsule.

verdict.reason: Sara Dosa delivers another visually stunning meditation that demands the big screen treatment. The Icelandic glacier cinematography creates an immersive experience that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: Aware that the future does not hold many options for her in a riverside community on Marajó Island, 13-year-old Marcielle decides to confront the violent machinery that governs her family and the women in her community.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to support emerging voices in Brazilian cinema, but the 101-minute runtime feels stretched for what amounts to a solid short film premise. Brennand shows real promise in capturing the texture of life on Marajó Island, but the pacing never quite justifies the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: An upper-class widow falls in love with a much younger, down-to-earth nurseryman, much to the disapproval of her children and criticism of her country club peers.

verdict.reason: Essential Sirk melodrama that defines the form with visual perfection and emotional complexity. The Technicolor cinematography transforms suburban conformity into a lush prison of mirrors and windows.

ratings.plot: This contemporary romantic comedy based on a global bestseller follows native New Yorker Rachel Chu to Singapore to meet her boyfriend's family.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need the communal rom-com experience tonight. The Singapore wedding sequence is the only part that truly benefits from theatrical viewing.

movies.title: Billie Eilish – Hit Me Hard and Soft: The Tour (Live in 3D)

ratings.plot: An accompanying 3D concert film, documenting the Manchester shows and co-directed by Eilish and James Cameron.

verdict.reason: See it in theaters for the full 3D spectacle that Cameron designed for the big screen. His technical mastery combined with Eilish's stage presence creates a concert experience that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: Loretta Castorini, a bookkeeper from Brooklyn, New York, finds herself in a difficult situation when she falls for the brother of the man she has agreed to marry.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Cher's Oscar-winning performance and one of the warmest romantic comedies ever made. Jewison captures the rhythms of Italian-American family life with perfect pitch and timing.

ratings.plot: Two disreputable friends get tied in with a group of criminals who turn out to be excessively violent and deceptive.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone tracking Hong Kong action cinema at its most unhinged. Ringo Lam directs with the same manic energy that made City on Fire a classic, but pushes the violence into genuinely disturbing territory.

ratings.plot: A horror writer visits an Irish inn to scatter his parents' ashes, unaware the property is said to be haunted by a witch.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need a solid horror fix tonight. Mc Carthy delivers effective scares but nothing that elevates above competent genre work.

ratings.plot: A senior chef lives with his three grown daughters; the middle one finds her future plans affected by unexpected events and the life changes of the other household members.

verdict.reason: See this while you can catch Lee's mastery of domestic drama on a big screen. The food preparation sequences alone justify the theatrical experience, with cinematography that makes every meal a sensual event.

ratings.plot: Showgirls Lorelei Lee and Dorothy Shaw travel to Paris, pursued by a private detective hired by the suspicious father of Lorelei's fiancé, as well as a rich, enamored old man and many other doting admirers.

ratings.plot: Nicky is on the run from the mob, and he turns to old pal Mikey for help.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most naturalistic crime films ever made. May's improvisational direction captures paranoia and friendship with a documentary intimacy that no other filmmaker has matched.

ratings.plot: After a young couple witnesses a gruesome highway accident, they soon realize they did not leave the crash scene alone, as a demonic presence called the Passenger won't stop until it claims them both.

verdict.reason: Decent possession horror but nothing urgent about catching it theatrically. Øvredal knows his craft but the scares work just as well at home.

ratings.plot: The accidental mix-up of four identical plaid overnight bags leads to a series of increasingly wild and wacky situations.

verdict.reason: This is screwball comedy done right, with Bogdanovich channeling Hawks at his sharpest. Streisand and O'Neal have the kind of rapid-fire chemistry that makes every line land like a punchline.

ratings.plot: A tale of nineteenth-century New York high society in which a young lawyer falls in love with a woman separated from her husband, while he is engaged to the woman's cousin.

verdict.reason: Essential Scorsese on the big screen where his camera work and Dante Ferretti's production design create the suffocating world of Gilded Age Manhattan. The film's visual storytelling about repressed desire works best in a theater where every glance and gesture fills the frame.

ratings.plot: A mean lord exiles fairytale creatures to the swamp of a grumpy ogre, who must go on a quest and rescue a princess for the lord in order to get his land back.

verdict.reason: Skip the standard screening when the movie party version offers the same film with added energy. Without the communal experience, this plays better at home where you can pause for snacks during the slower middle act.

movies.title: The Last One for the Road

ratings.plot: Two blustery 50-somethings, have an obsession: going for their last drink. One night, they run into Giulio, a shy architecture student, whose way of seeing the world and love will be transformed as the trio wander from bar to bar ...

verdict.reason: This Italian bar-hopping character study has warmth but lacks the urgency for tonight's ticket. Sossai keeps the wandering focused enough that the 98-minute runtime never drags.

ratings.plot: When Elizabeth Bennet meets the handsome Mr. Darcy, she believes he is the last man she could ever marry, but as their lives become intertwined, she finds herself captivated by the man she has sworn to hate forever.

verdict.reason: Wright's adaptation deserves the big screen treatment for its sweeping cinematography and Knightley's magnetic performance. The film transforms Austen's drawing room drama into something genuinely cinematic through its use of natural light and landscape.

ratings.plot: Three film students vanish after traveling into a Maryland forest to film a documentary on the local Blair Witch legend, leaving only their footage behind.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for its revolutionary found footage technique that spawned countless imitators. The handheld camera work creates genuine dread through what you never see rather than cheap scares.

ratings.plot: Hathaway Noa, still haunted by his past actions, joins a terrorist organization called Mufti and adopts the alias "Mufti Nabiyy Erin". Mufti is fighting a terrorist campaign against the Earth Federation government.

verdict.reason: See this on the biggest screen possible for the spectacular mecha battles. The animation quality represents the highest tier of what modern Gundam can achieve in theaters.

ratings.plot: In the tense 72 hours before D-Day, General Dwight D. Eisenhower and Captain James Stagg face an impossible choice--launch the most dangerous seaborne invasion in history or risk losing the war altogether.

verdict.reason: Wait for wider release unless you need your World War II drama fix tonight. Maras proved he can handle tense real-time scenarios with Hotel Mumbai, but this D-Day weather forecasting premise feels more suited to prestige television than theatrical urgency.

ratings.plot: A group of very different individuals staying at a luxurious hotel in Berlin deal with each of their respective dramas.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for the only Best Picture winner with no other Oscar nominations. Goulding's revolving door structure influenced decades of ensemble filmmaking that followed.

ratings.plot: While passing through a vacation resort, a newlywed couple encounters a mysterious, strikingly beautiful countess and her aide.

verdict.reason: See it for one of the most seductive vampire films ever made. Kümel's visual approach treats blood and sexuality as elegant design elements rather than exploitation.

ratings.plot: In 1924, Oskar Matzerath is born in the Free City of Danzig. At age three, he falls down a flight of stairs and stops growing. In 1939, World War II breaks out.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Schlöndorff's bold adaptation of Grass's impossible novel. The film's grotesque imagery and Bennent's fearless child performance create a theatrical experience that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: When his family goes away for summer vacation, a hitherto faithful husband with an overactive imagination is tempted by a sexy new neighbor.

verdict.reason: Essential Wilder that plays better with a crowd than at home. Marilyn Monroe's subway grate scene was shot on this very street corner and the film captures her at peak magnetism.

ratings.plot: After a car wreck, an amnesiac woman sets out to learn what happened to her with the help of a wannabe actress.

verdict.reason: Essential Lynch on the big screen where his sound design and dream logic hit hardest. The film's fractured narrative rewards the focused attention that only a theater provides.

ratings.plot: A strange doorway appears in the basement of a furniture showroom.

verdict.reason: Worth checking out if you're drawn to liminal space horror, but this feels more like an extended YouTube video than essential cinema. Parsons brings his viral found footage sensibility to feature length without quite justifying the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: Gianfranco Rosi's new documentary is an immersive portrait of those trying to survive in the war-torn Middle East.

verdict.reason: Rosi delivers his most powerful work yet, capturing the human cost of Middle Eastern conflict with unflinching intimacy. His patient observational style transforms 100 minutes into something close to a prayer for the displaced and forgotten.

ratings.plot: A young student is devastated when he finds that his girlfriend is cheating on him. In order to find out why she did it, he decides to spy on her and her lover.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you have patience for Rohmer's conversational rhythms and microscopic emotional observations. The 106-minute runtime will test viewers who need more than café philosophy and sidewalk surveillance to stay engaged.

ratings.plot: The crew of a bankrupt Scottish trawler turn to smuggling illegal immigrants over the stormy waters of the North Sea.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the gritty maritime atmosphere, but the immigration thriller feels more urgent on streaming than in theaters. Hudson captures the claustrophobic desperation of the trawler with documentary-like precision.

ratings.plot: When a group of trespassing seniors swim in a pool containing alien cocoons, they find themselves energized with youthful vigor.

verdict.reason: Skip it unless you need comfort food cinema that delivers exactly what the premise promises. Howard directs with workmanlike efficiency that never rises above the material's Hallmark sentimentality.

movies.title: In Viaggio: The Travels of Pope Francis

ratings.plot: In the first nine years of his pontificate, Pope Francis made 37 trips visiting 53 countries, focusing on his most important issues: poverty, migration, the environment, solidarity and war. Intrigued by the fact that two of Franci...

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you connect with Rosi's observational style, but this feels more like a respectful dispatch than essential cinema. The director keeps his usual distance, which works better for his border films than for papal portraiture.

ratings.plot: Three women set out to find eligible millionaires to marry, but find true love in the process.

verdict.reason: See this CinemaScope pioneer on the biggest screen you can find. The wide-format photography that defined 1950s moviegoing deserves proper theatrical presentation.

ratings.plot: In pre-revolutionary Russia, a Jewish peasant with traditional values contends with marrying off three of his daughters with modern romantic ideals while growing anti-Semitic sentiment threatens his village.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you can handle three hours and have never seen it theatrically. The musical numbers gain power on a big screen but the runtime tests patience.

ratings.plot: Tommy is an unemployed mechanic who spends most of his time in a bar (Trees Lounge) in a small blue collar town. He seems to always be thinking, "If only X then I could stop drinking".

verdict.reason: Buscemi's directorial debut works better as a late-night discovery than an essential theatrical trip. The bar-stool philosophy and small-town ennui benefit from the intimate focus only a lead actor-director can provide.

ratings.plot: Three hoodlums carefully case a small town while planning to rob the bank on the upcoming Saturday. On Saturday, things turn violent and deadly.

verdict.reason: Fleischer transforms a simple heist setup into a masterclass of tension and character study. The CinemaScope photography makes every frame feel claustrophobic despite the wide open desert setting.

ratings.plot: After a young man gets separated from his friends in the woods, he falls in to a 10 foot deep pit of spikes, impaling him through his leg and leaving him trapped. He quickly learns that his fall was not an accident.

verdict.reason: Skip this derivative survival horror that offers nothing new to the trapped-in-a-hole subgenre. The premise exhausts itself in the first act and never recovers.

ratings.plot: As two couples are visiting Niagara Falls, tensions between one wife and her husband reach the level of murder.

verdict.reason: Catch this Technicolor showcase that uses the Falls as more than scenery. Hathaway turns the tourist destination into a character that amplifies the noir tension.

ratings.plot: As Miranda Priestly nears retirement, she reunites with Andy Sachs to face off against her former assistant turned rival: Emily Charlton.

verdict.reason: A solid sequel that lacks the original's bite but delivers enough workplace comedy to justify the ticket price. Streep elevates familiar material with a more vulnerable take on Miranda, though the fashion industry satire feels less urgent twenty years later.

ratings.plot: For nearly two years of exploring the "Great Ring Road's" almost 70km of looping highway, Gianfranco Rosi brings to the foreground the daily routine of ordinary people, composing the profile of a microcosm on the outskirts of Grea...

verdict.reason: Rosi's documentary experiment works as ambient cinema but lacks the urgency for tonight's ticket. The highway meditation benefits more from patient home viewing than theatrical commitment.

ratings.plot: A fearless, globe-trotting, terrorist-battling secret agent has his life turned upside down when he discovers his wife might be having an affair with a used-car salesman while terrorists smuggle nuclear war heads into the United S...

verdict.reason: See it for the practical effects and helicopter stunts if you can tolerate the misogyny. Cameron's action choreography still impresses but the gender politics feel fossilized.

ratings.plot: In the 1980s, a determined criminal-minded Cuban immigrant becomes the biggest drug smuggler in Miami, and is eventually undone by his own drug addiction.

verdict.reason: De Palma's operatic gangster epic works best for those who can handle three hours of pure excess. The cocaine-fueled madness benefits from theatrical scale, but the runtime demands serious commitment.

ratings.plot: Police Inspector Pao is trying to catch Mak Kwan, a gang member who is first arrested, but then escapes from the prison. By chance, Pao realizes that the target of Kwan's gang is the H. K. Jockey Club.

verdict.reason: Ringo Lam delivers one of the tightest police procedurals of the 1990s Hong Kong wave. The cat-and-mouse structure builds to a finale that justifies the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: A woman cannot decide between two men who love her, and the trio agree to try living together in a platonic friendly relationship.

verdict.reason: Lubitsch transforms Noel Coward's play into pure cinema gold with innuendo that still feels dangerous. The sophistication and wit reward big-screen attention to every gesture.

ratings.plot: A teenage English boy struggles to survive with his fellow captives under Japanese occupation of China during World War II.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the airplane sequences and Ballard's Shanghai, but only if you have not experienced it before. Spielberg's spectacle works better theatrically than the coming-of-age story.

ratings.plot: Desolate by the loss of his daughter, a geneticist creates a monstrous new mutation.
ratings.director: Kazuki Ômori, Kôji Hashimoto, Kenjirô Ohmori

verdict.reason: The most beautiful monster movie ever made deserves the biggest screen possible. The practical effects and botanical horror imagery create visuals that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: A chemist finds his personal and professional life turned upside down when one of his chimpanzees finds the fountain of youth.

verdict.reason: Hawks delivers solid screwball mechanics but this ranks among his minor efforts. The fountain-of-youth premise never generates the energy of his best comedies.

ratings.plot: A naive but stubborn cowboy falls in love with a saloon singer and tries to take her away against her will to get married and live on his ranch in Montana.

verdict.reason: Monroe gives one of her best dramatic performances but the cowboy romance feels dated. The acting elevates material that now plays more problematic than charming.

ratings.plot: Set on the French Riviera in the summer of 1915, Jean Renoir -- son of the Impressionist painter, Pierre-Auguste -- returns home to convalesce after being wounded in World War I. At his side is Andrée, a young woman who rejuvenate...

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to period pieces about artistic dynasties, but it's more admirable than engaging. Bourdieu brings visual elegance to the Côte d'Azur setting but the pacing never finds its rhythm.

ratings.plot: After a decade in prison for her sister's murder, Elisa barely remembers the crime. A criminologist helps her uncover buried memories, leading her toward potential redemption.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you trust Di Costanzo's careful hand with psychological material. The director's previous work proves he can find genuine emotion in damaged characters without exploitation.

ratings.plot: A White couple's attitudes are challenged when their daughter introduces them to her Black fiancé.

verdict.reason: This Oscar winner feels more like a liberal lecture than a movie. The social relevance that once made it essential now exposes how cautiously it approached its own subject.

ratings.plot: An extremely pampered African prince travels to Queens, New York and goes undercover to find a wife that he can respect for her intelligence and strong will.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need Murphy's barber shop scenes on a big screen tonight. Landis keeps the energy uneven between Murphy's showcase moments and the thin romantic plot.

ratings.plot: While recovering in Venice, sickly composer Gustav von Aschenbach becomes dangerously fixated with teenager Tadzio.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Visconti's mastery of decay and desire rendered through impossibly lush cinematography. The Mann adaptation transforms literary obsession into pure visual poetry that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: The problematic lives of teenager students for whom the singer Lily Chou-Chou's dreamy music is the only way to escape an alienating, violent and insensitive society.

verdict.reason: Worth the 146-minute commitment if you connect with Iwai's dreamy blend of teen alienation and ethereal soundscapes. The film works best for viewers willing to absorb its mood rather than follow traditional narrative structure.

ratings.plot: A new teacher at a troubled inner-city high school soon ends up clashing with a delinquent punk posse who run the institute with an iron fist.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want unapologetic 80s exploitation that takes its violence seriously. Lester directs with genuine menace rather than camp, making this nastier and more effective than most high school revenge fantasies.

ratings.plot: New project from Avalon Fast. A story of impossible redemption, modern witchcraft, and duels that repeat themselves like cursed cycles.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to Fast's singular vision of occult violence, but this feels like homework more than entertainment. The film's recursive structure becomes genuinely hypnotic in the final act, though it demands serious patience to get there.

ratings.plot: The fan favorite champions -- now joined by Johnny Cage himself -- are pitted against one another in the ultimate battle to defeat the dark rule of Shao Kahn that threatens the very existence of the Earthrealm and its defenders.

verdict.reason: Wait for opening weekend reactions before buying a ticket to this video game sequel. McQuoid's first Mortal Kombat delivered solid fights but weak character work, making this follow-up a gamble despite the Johnny Cage addition.

ratings.plot: Young blind Sarah is staying with relatives in their English countryside manor. An unknown maniac enters the house and murders all of Sarah's relatives. When the culprit realizes that Sarah is still alive, he pursues her.

verdict.reason: Fleischer delivers solid craftsmanship in a basic cat-and-mouse premise that works better as a rental. The blind protagonist angle adds tension but the 89-minute thriller lacks the theatrical punch to justify the trip.

ratings.plot: Mae Doyle comes back to her hometown a cynical woman. Her brother Joe fears that his love, fish cannery worker Peggy, may wind up like Mae. Mae marries Jerry and has a baby; she is happy but restless, drawn to Jerry's friend Earl.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Lang completists and anyone interested in his American noir period. Barbara Stanwyck delivers one of her most complex performances as a woman caught between desire and domesticity.

ratings.plot: Cujo, a friendly St. Bernard, contracts rabies and conducts a reign of terror on an American farm.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need genuine tension on a big screen. Teague builds real dread in that car, but the film works just as well at home.

ratings.plot: The titular river unites a farmer recently released from prison, his young son and an ambitious saloon singer. In order to survive, each must be purged of anger, and each must learn to understand and care for the others.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for one of the most beautiful color westerns ever shot. Preminger turns the CinemaScope wilderness into a character that dwarfs Mitchum and Monroe's considerable screen presence.

movies.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu

ratings.plot: Once a lone bounty hunter, Mandalorian Din Djarin and his apprentice Grogu embark on an exciting new Star Wars adventure.

verdict.reason: Only worth seeing if you need your Grogu fix on the biggest screen possible. Favreau directs competently but this feels like an extended TV episode that costs twenty dollars instead of your Disney Plus subscription.

ratings.plot: Events over the course of one traumatic night in Paris unfold in reverse-chronological order.

verdict.reason: Only worth the trip if you can handle Noé's most punishing work without walking out. The reverse chronology structure and 13-minute single-take rape scene make this an endurance test that demands big-screen commitment.

ratings.plot: Two American newlyweds in Paris experience a love so strong, it almost devours them.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Denis devotees who can handle genuinely disturbing material about desire and consumption. The film pushes cannibalistic metaphor to literal extremes that make most horror films look tame.

ratings.plot: Sentenced to life imprisonment for illegal activities, Italian International member Giulio Manieri holds on to his political ideals while struggling against madness in the loneliness of his prison cell.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Taviani completists but this early political prison drama lacks the visual poetry of their later masterworks. The brothers hadn't yet found their distinctive voice, making it more of historical interest than essential viewing.

ratings.plot: The Top Gun Naval Fighter Weapons School is where the best of the best train to refine their elite flying skills. When hotshot fighter pilot Maverick is sent to the school, his reckless attitude and cocky demeanor put him at odds ...

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want the full-throttle fighter jet spectacle on the biggest screen possible. The aerial sequences still deliver genuine thrills that streaming flattens into nothing.

ratings.plot: A woman advertising for a new roommate finds that something very strange is going on with the tenant who decides to move in.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you want expertly crafted 90s thriller nastiness that knows exactly what it is. Schroeder directs with the surgical precision he brought to Reversal of Fortune, turning apartment living into genuine claustrophobia.

ratings.plot: When her brother decides to ditch for a couple weeks, Viola heads over to his elite boarding school, disguised as him, and proceeds to fall for his school's star soccer player, and soon learns she's not the only one with romantic ...

verdict.reason: Worth a watch if you need a comfort-food gender-swap comedy that actually commits to its Shakespearean source. Amanda Bynes carries the entire film with genuine comic timing that elevates the familiar formula.

ratings.plot: Corrupt Atlantic City police officer Rick Santoro and Navy Commander Kevin Dunn join forces to investigate both the assassination of Secretary of Defense Charles Kirkland and the disappearance of a beautiful stranger.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for De Palma's trademark long takes and split-screen work, but the third act collapses under its own convoluted plotting. The opening 20-minute sequence shot is a masterclass in camera movement and staging.

ratings.plot: Two terrible lounge singers get booked to play a gig in a Moroccan hotel but somehow become pawns in an international power play between the C.I.A., the Emir of Ishtar, and the rebels trying to overthrow his regime.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you appreciate the ambition behind notorious flops, but the comedy rarely connects despite strong craft. May's direction is more interesting than funny, turning what should be slapstick into something oddly contemplative.

ratings.plot: An 18-year-old female amateur dancer who performs nightly at a dancing bar, and works as a welder during the day, dreams of joining the Pittsburgh ballet school.

verdict.reason: Pure 80s artifice that works best as a time capsule experience. Lyne's music video aesthetics and the iconic water dance sequence deserve the big screen treatment.

ratings.plot: Corporate executives on a team-building retreat face a deadly struggle when their leader turns violently against them.

verdict.reason: Watch if you enjoy workplace satire that actually understands corporate absurdity. The ensemble cast finds genuine laughs in familiar territory without resorting to broad caricature.

movies.title: Aloïse preceded by Qui donc a rêvé?

verdict.reason: Worth it if you have patience for Liliane de Kermadec's patient character study of the outsider artist. The pairing with the short film suggests this is curated for viewers ready to spend time with difficult subjects.

Worth it if you want to see Cézanne's late paintings discussed in their historical context. Danièle Huillet and Jean-Marie Straub turn a museum lecture into rigorous cinema through their signature static shots and direct sound recording.
Essential viewing for Takahata's devastating wartime masterpiece in its original Japanese. The film's hand-drawn animation achieves an emotional weight that demands theatrical presentation.

Worth checking out if you have preview access, but this feels more like a festival curiosity than essential viewing. The members-only format suggests limited wider release, making this your main chance to see it theatrically.

ratings.plot: During a five year period an Italian filmmaker documents the world of down-on-their-luck individuals who live in a Californian desert trying to get by one day at a time. None of them has more than a vehicle, a dog and some clothes.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in observational documentary at its purest. Rosi spent five years gaining access to this invisible community, and the patience shows in every frame.

ratings.plot: Boatman is Gianfranco Rosi's account of a boattrip along the Ganges river, together with his helmsman Gopal. They pass tourists and Indians who are bathing, working or meditating. In a series of small portraits Rosi depicts life o...

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you connect with slow observational cinema, but the 55-minute runtime and meditative pace make this better suited for a quiet evening than an urgent theatrical trip. Rosi's patient eye captures moments along the Ganges that reward close attention but demand the right mood.
Essential Cronenberg on 35mm at a venue that programs thoughtfully. The body horror imagery hits harder on the big screen than any home format can deliver.

ratings.plot: When a college professor confronts two hunters she catches trespassing on her property, she's drawn into an escalating battle of wills with catastrophic consequences.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for Thandiwe Newton's fierce lead performance, but the slow-burn escalation drags in the middle act. Higgins builds genuine tension through the Montana wilderness setting, making every encounter feel dangerously isolated.

movies.title: Grand Budapest Hotel preceded by Hotel Chevalier

verdict.reason: See Anderson's masterpiece on the big screen with the rare theatrical pairing of his Darjeeling Limited short. The double feature creates a perfect evening of his signature dollhouse aesthetic at proper cinema scale.

ratings.plot: Several stories depicting the landscapes and fauna of India are mixed with documentary footage.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want Rossellini's outsider perspective on India, but this feels more like commissioned travelogue than essential cinema. The mix of staged drama and documentary footage never coheres into the kind of unified vision that makes his Italian films indispensable.

Skip unless you're specifically drawn to contemporary African cinema from emerging voices. The limited information suggests this is worth supporting for cultural representation but may lack the theatrical urgency for a general recommendation.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Worth it if you're already familiar with Malle's work, but too narrow for casual viewing. At 65 minutes, Duguet keeps the focus tight on the filmmaker's political evolution without padding.

ratings.plot: A young Vietnamse girl must find her lost family after her city was destroyed by US's bombing campaign in 1972.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for the historical perspective but lacks the cinematic power to make it essential tonight. Hai Ninh's intimate 72-minute runtime keeps the focus tight on personal trauma within wartime devastation.

movies.title: This Long Century presents Ben Rivers

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in experimental cinema's relationship to landscape and time. Rivers' 16mm work transforms ordinary rural spaces into something approaching the mythic through patient, static compositions that reward theatrical attention.

ratings.plot: A Hong Kong Special Branch cop and a CIA agent reluctantly team up to bring down a major international terrorist.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you want peak-era Hong Kong action before Ringo Lam's Hollywood detour. The gunplay and stunt coordination hit harder than most American counterparts from the same period.
Skip this one unless you have a specific reason to see this particular screening. Not enough information available to recommend the theatrical experience over other viewing options.
Worth seeing if you connect with intimate character studies, but the pacing demands patience. The open captioning makes this accessible viewing for a broader audience tonight.

Worth seeing if you're drawn to experimental narrative structures, but the pacing tests patience. The open captioning format makes this accessible while preserving the film's deliberate sound design choices.

ratings.plot: A stand-up comedy special from Akaash Singh, known for his work on the Flagrant podcast.

verdict.reason: Skip this overlong comedy special that stretches thin material to feature length. Singh works better in podcast format where his timing feels more natural.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need a guaranteed crowd-pleaser for a light night out. The communal laughter hits differently in a theater full of cat people than it does on your phone.

ratings.plot: 90-year-old Agatha Bock maintains her ancestral farm and heirloom seeds without modern amenities, preserving traditional practices through daily routines that document a fading way of life.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you have patience for observational documentary about vanishing rural traditions. The 90-year-old subject's commitment to preserving heirloom seeds and pre-modern farming methods creates genuine documentary intimacy.

ratings.plot: A New York fashion model finds herself being pursued by a poor but honest garage mechanic and a rich philanderer.
ratings.director: Alexander Hall, David Burton, William C. de Mille

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you enjoy early 1930s melodrama, but this is minor studio work that lacks the bite of better pre-Code films. The 70-minute runtime keeps things moving, but the love triangle never rises above routine Hollywood formula.
Worth it for animation historians but too narrow for a general night out. The archival footage showcases Max Fleischer's pre-Code ingenuity before sanitization neutered the character.

ratings.plot: It’s the bubbly and hilarious legal comedy that taught us to never judge a book by its fabulous look, and more importantly, how to bend and snap. Elle Woods (Reese Witherspoon), a fashionable sorority queen, is dumped by her dream boyfriend. She decides the way back into his heart is to follow him to law school, but she soon finds something far more rewarding than her bonehead ex as she exceeds all the stuck-up expectations. Witherspoon shines as the tough but always effervescent Elle in this bleached-out comedy about not only proving your worth but doing it for yourself. Just remember to keep that perm dry. Wouldn’t want to deactivate the ammonium thioglycolate.

verdict.reason: See Witherspoon's star-making performance on the big screen where it belongs — this comedy holds up beautifully. The anniversary screening celebrates a film that became more culturally important than anyone expected in 2001.

ratings.plot: Follows ASCO, the Los Angeles based avant-garde art group during the 1970s and '80s.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to Chicano art history, but the 90-minute runtime suggests a surface-level survey. Gutiérrez Senger's direction will determine whether this feels essential or like an extended museum exhibit.

Worth it if you can handle five hours of Bertolucci's epic ambitions about class warfare in fascist Italy. The runtime demands serious commitment, but the sweeping cinematography and De Niro-Depardieu performances justify the theatrical experience when you're in the right mood.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Hard to recommend without knowing more about this debut feature from Nicolangelo Gelormini. The complete lack of available information suggests limited distribution or festival-only status.

ratings.plot: An adaption of Claudio Monteverdis opera.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you're drawn to experimental narrative structures that prioritize mood over conventional storytelling. The film's deliberate pacing and abstract approach to the Orpheus myth rewards patience but lacks the urgency that makes theatrical viewing essential.

A retrospective screening worth your time if you're ready for Rossellini's deliberate pacing and moral complexity. The theatrical experience depends entirely on which specific film is showing and the quality of the print.

ratings.plot: In the 1800s, after an assassination attempt by Prince Ramon against the king of Mandorra, a brigand resembling the king surreptitiously impersonates the incapacitated monarch in order to throw off the plotters.

verdict.reason: Phil Karlson's swashbuckler has moments of genuine tension but lacks the budget to match its ambitions. The dual role premise gives Anthony Dexter more to work with than his usual leading man parts.
Only essential if you are tracking international arthouse releases. The premise is stronger than the execution and the runtime drags.
Worth it for completists of the genre but not essential viewing tonight. The execution is competent without being memorable.

movies.title: Masters of the Universe (2026) Cereal Party

ratings.plot: Director Travis Knight brings the legendary franchise back to the big screen in this epic live-action adventure. After being separated for 15 years, the Sword of Power leads Prince Adam (Nicholas Galitzine) back to Eternia, where he discovers his home shattered under the fiendish rule of Skeletor (Jared Leto). To save his family and his world, Adam must join forces with his closest allies, Teela (Camila Mendes) and Duncan/Man-At-Arms (Idris Elba), and embrace his true destiny as He-Man – the most powerful man in the universe.

verdict.reason: Wait to see if Knight can bring the Kubo magic to live-action franchise work. Leto as Skeletor either works completely or becomes an instant meme, with no middle ground possible.

ratings.plot: The toys are back in Disney and Pixar’s TOY STORY 5, and this time around, it’s Toy meets Tech. Buzz, Woody, Jessie, and the rest of the gang’s jobs get exponentially harder when they go head-to-head with this all-new threat to playtime. It’s been seven years since the last adventure, and the whole crew returns to face a world where imagination competes with something a little more...automated. This new chapter brings the gang back together for a story about loyalty, change, and what happens when play evolves faster than the toys can keep up.
Woody, Buzz, Jessie and the rest of the gang's jobs are challenged when they're introduced to electronics, a new threat to playtime.

verdict.reason: Wait to see if this latest sequel justifies its existence beyond franchise obligation. The electronics angle feels like a forced update to a story that already found its perfect ending twice.

ratings.plot: Presented in Japanese with English subtitles Come and join Crunchyroll for an exclusive sneak peek at the new July season, BEFORE it hits streaming. We will be screening select episodes of new seasons for the first time. A can’t-miss theatrical event!

verdict.reason: Only essential if you follow seasonal anime religiously and want bragging rights. The theatrical exclusivity matters to hardcore fans, but casual viewers can wait for the streaming release without missing anything crucial.

ratings.plot: If you found out we weren't alone, if someone showed you, proved it to you, would that frighten you?

verdict.reason: Spielberg tackling alien contact is worth the trip for any serious moviegoer. The director's mastery of wonder and dread makes this the rare sci-fi thriller that demands the big screen experience.

ratings.plot: Amber and her four best friends flee Los Angeles for a girls' trip in Joshua Tree, only to find themselves unwelcome in a desert town simmering with quiet hostility. As isolation sets in and encounters with aggressive locals grow more threatening, festering resentments within the group begin to surface. What begins as a fun and reckless escape spirals into a violent struggle for control and survival, as past wounds and present dangers collide in a night that turns their trip into a revenge-fueled nightmare.

verdict.reason: Worth attending if you want to hear the filmmakers defend their choices in person. The live Q&A offers the chance to understand what went wrong in this messy desert thriller that mistakes repetitive hostility for mounting tension.

ratings.plot: Coley, 17, from rural Oregon, navigates intimacy after her mother's passing. Meeting Sonya sparks new feelings, but self-doubt hinders their connection. Sonya, unfamiliar with dating girls, is uncertain. They learn to embrace emot...

verdict.reason: Wait for reviews before buying tickets to this debut feature. Kiyoko's music video background suggests strong visual instincts, but first-time directors often struggle with feature pacing.

movies.title: Girls Like Girls with Livestream Q&A

ratings.plot: Based on writer/director Hayley Kiyoko's hit single and best-selling novel of the same name and featuring all-new music from Kiyoko, GIRLS LIKE GIRLS is a heartfelt coming-of-age story set over the course of one sun-drenched summer, where new-girl-in-town Coley falls in love for the first time while learning to accept herself along the way.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you connect with Kiyoko's music or need a gentle coming-of-age story tonight. The livestream Q&A adds value, but the film itself plays safer than its pop-star origins suggest.

ratings.plot: Shrouded in secrecy! Encircled by intrigue! The Mystery Machine promises to deliver cinematic treasures that span genres, countries, and tastes... only for those brave enough to enter. All shall be revealed only after the credits begin. Screening on select Mondays with discounted admission.

verdict.reason: Take the gamble only if you enjoy theatrical surprises and can handle potential disappointment. The discounted Monday screening softens the financial risk of this programming gimmick.

ratings.plot: Tells the story of the six wives of Henry VIII, reimagining them as pop stars who reclaim their narratives from their infamous husband's shadow.

verdict.reason: Worth catching if you need a Broadway fix without the Broadway prices. The pop concert format works better live than it will on eventual streaming platforms.

movies.title: Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby 20th Anniversary Re-Release

ratings.plot: Lifelong friends and national idols Ricky Bobby and Cal Naughton Jr. have earned their NASCAR stripes with their uncanny knack of finishing races in the first and second slots, respectively, and slinging catchphrases like "Shake and bake!" But when a rival French driver coasts onto the track to challenge their records, they will have to floor it to retain their top-dog status!

verdict.reason: Skip the anniversary lap unless you need the big screen energy for McKay's NASCAR satire. The theatrical experience adds little that streaming cannot deliver for this comedy.

movies.title: Masters of the Universe (2026) HDR by Barco Early Access

ratings.plot: Director Travis Knight brings the legendary franchise back to the big screen in this epic live-action adventure. After being separated for 15 years, the Sword of Power leads Prince Adam (Nicholas Galitzine) back to Eternia, where he discovers his home shattered under the fiendish rule of Skeletor (Jared Leto). To save his family and his world, Adam must join forces with his closest allies, Teela (Camila Mendes) and Duncan/Man-At-Arms (Idris Elba), and embrace his true destiny as He-Man – the most powerful man in the universe.

verdict.reason: Pass on this preview screening of what looks like another hollow franchise revival. Knight's stop-motion expertise does not translate to live-action spectacle, and 134 minutes is too long for plastic sword mythology.

ratings.plot: The toys are back in Disney and Pixar’s TOY STORY 5, and this time around, it’s Toy meets Tech. Buzz, Woody, Jessie, and the rest of the gang’s jobs get exponentially harder when they go head-to-head with this all-new threat to playtime. It’s been seven years since the last adventure, and the whole crew returns to face a world where imagination competes with something a little more...automated. This new chapter brings the gang back together for a story about loyalty, change, and what happens when play evolves faster than the toys can keep up.

verdict.reason: Skip this franchise cashgrab that adds nothing new to a story that ended perfectly with the third film. The HDR projection cannot salvage a script that feels like a corporate mandate rather than creative necessity.

movies.title: Girls Like Girls with Live Q&A

ratings.plot: Based on writer/director Hayley Kiyoko's hit single and best-selling novel of the same name and featuring all-new music from Kiyoko, GIRLS LIKE GIRLS is a heartfelt coming-of-age story set over the course of one sun-drenched summer, where new-girl-in-town Coley falls in love for the first time while learning to accept herself along the way.

verdict.reason: The live Q&A with Hayley Kiyoko adds value for fans, but the film itself feels like an extended music video stretched thin. Kiyoko's earnest direction can't overcome the lightweight storytelling that never deepens beyond its YA source material.

movies.title: Gregg Allman: The Music of my Soul

ratings.plot: Documentary that chronicles the life and origins of one of the original Allman Brothers Band founders.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need a straightforward Allman Brothers primer tonight. Director James Keach delivers competent but unremarkable music documentary work that lacks the edge of its subject.

movies.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu HDR by Barco

ratings.plot: STAR WARS: THE MANDALORIAN AND GROGU contains several sequences with flashing lights that may affect those who are susceptible to photosensitive epilepsy or have other photosensitivities. The evil Empire has fallen, and Imperial warlords remain scattered throughout the galaxy. As the fledgling New Republic works to protect everything the Rebellion fought for, they have enlisted the help of legendary Mandalorian bounty hunter Din Djarin (Pedro Pascal) and his young apprentice Grogu.

verdict.reason: Wait for reviews before committing to this franchise extension. The HDR presentation offers superior image quality, but two hours feels long for what amounts to a TV spinoff stretched to feature length.

ratings.plot: Presented in Spanish with English subtitles Mexico City. A fatal car crash. Three lives collide. A teenager risks it all to run away with his brother’s wife. A model loses everything after moving in with her lover. A homeless man unearths the ghosts of his past. As their stories unfold, human chaos, cruelty, and tenderness come to light – revealing they are more connected than they seem.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most visceral debuts in modern cinema. Iñárritu's breakneck editing and raw Mexico City locations demand the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: An author returns to his hometown of Jerusalem's Lot in search of inspiration for his next book, only to discover that the townspeople are being attacked by a bloodthirsty vampire.

verdict.reason: Skip this hollow adaptation that wastes King's material on generic vampire scares. Dauberman's direction lacks the atmospheric dread that made the 1979 Hooper version essential viewing.

movies.title: The Snake Girl and the Silver-Haired Witch

ratings.plot: After years at a Catholic orphanage, a young girl's life takes an unexpected turn when her long-absent father suddenly appears to reclaim her.

verdict.reason: Worth checking out if you're drawn to Japanese B-horror from the late sixties. Yuasa's direction leans heavily on atmospheric Gothic imagery that works better in a theater than at home.

movies.title: BTS World Tour 'ARIRANG' in Busan: Live Viewing

ratings.plot: The historic journey continues. Following the record-breaking opening of the ‘ARIRANG’ world tour, pop royalty BTS returns to the iconic Busan Asiad Main Stadium for a monumental homecoming, broadcast live to cinemas around the world. This marks their poignant return to the very venue where they shared their final full-group performance before enlistment in 3 years and 8 months. Spanning 34 cities with 85 shows, this tour sets a new pinnacle as the most extensive tour ever by a Korean artist. Most notably, the performance falls on June 13 – BTS’s debut anniversary – adding a profound layer of symbolism as the group celebrates their legacy and future. Titled ARIRANG, the tour accompanies BTS’s fifth full-length album, weaving together the group’s identity through honest introspection and the universal themes of longing and deep love. With its signature spectacular 360-degree in-the-round stage design, the show delivers an immersive experience that places you at the heart of the celebration. Don’t miss this defining cultural moment. Share the electrifying energy of BTS’ stage on the big screen worldwide on a special day, June 13, from Busan.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need the communal energy of a live broadcast with fellow fans. The 195-minute runtime and cinema viewing format make this more of an endurance test than an essential theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: If you found out we weren’t alone, if someone showed you, proved it to you, would that frighten you? This summer, the truth belongs to eight billion people, and it’s arriving from the mind and lens of the greatest filmmaker of our time. In DISCLOSURE DAY, a strange signal changes everything, and suddenly the whole world is asking if we’re alone. It’s a big, eerie, all-eyes-on-the-sky kind of sci-fi event, and it’s coming from the greatest filmmaker of our time, someone who knows a thing or two about extra terrestrials…

verdict.reason: Skip this obvious marketing stunt masquerading as cinema. The vague plot description and breathless promotional language suggest a product, not a film worth your evening.

movies.title: The Amazing Circus: The Last Act

ratings.plot: Feature length version of the final two episodes (episode 8 and 9) of The Amazing Digital Circus, showing the conclusion of the cast of characters trapped in a digital realm under the mercy of an unstable ringmaster.

verdict.reason: Only worth the trip if you are already invested in this digital animation series. The feature-length conclusion combines two episodes for theatrical exclusivity, but this feels designed for existing fans rather than newcomers.

movies.title: I’m the Same, I'm an Other

ratings.plot: The Biblical story of Salomé, a girl who agrees to perform the "dance of the seven veils" in return for John the Baptist's head on a silver platter.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to experimental dance cinema, but this feels more suited to a gallery than a theater. The film's static compositions and repetitive structure test patience over its feature length.

ratings.plot: Inspired by Christian mythology, from which it draws a creative transformation force, in a search for identity that questions the two cultures to which the filmmaker belonged.

verdict.reason: Only worth seeing if you're already deep into experimental cinema or have a specific interest in the director's work. The pacing demands patience that most viewers won't have on a weeknight.

ratings.plot: A beautiful, wealthy woman's insatiable appetite for romance leads to tragedy and a police investigation.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to intimate character studies, but this won't be essential viewing for most. The film's strength lies in its naturalistic approach to documenting a young woman's emotional journey without forced dramatic beats.

Worth attending if you're interested in Hong Kong video art history, but this is specialized programming. Pau's pioneering work with early digital media deserves the institutional spotlight at this rare retrospective screening.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: The Jaguar Speaks the Language of Flowers

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you want to engage with experimental cinema that demands patience and attention. Hernández's Super 8 work creates hypnotic visual textures that benefit from theatrical projection but will frustrate anyone expecting conventional narrative structure.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: A Journal Is a Measure of Semen in a Die

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in underground queer cinema, but the fragmentary diary structure demands patience with nonlinear storytelling. Hernández's Super 8 work captures raw intimacy in ways that feel genuinely transgressive rather than merely provocative.

movies.title: Teo Hernández: Cluster of Eyes—Michel Nedjar, Jakobois, and MétroBarbèsRochechou Art

verdict.reason: Worth seeing if you're drawn to experimental French cinema from the margins. Hernández's handheld Super 8 work creates an intensely personal visual language that demands patience and rewards attention.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Worth it if you're drawn to experimental documentary work that prioritizes atmosphere over narrative. The film's meditative pacing and abstract structure make it better suited for patient viewers seeking immersive visual poetry.
Worth the trip for one of the most visually stunning anime films ever made. The hand-drawn animation combined with early CGI creates a unique aesthetic that demands the biggest screen possible.

ratings.plot: Chronicles the intertwined lives of engineering students as they navigate academic challenges, forge friendships, and discover themselves in university.

verdict.reason: Skip this predictable campus drama that offers nothing new to the crowded coming-of-age genre. The 135-minute runtime stretches thin material past the breaking point.
Worth your time if personal documentaries about ordinary lives resonate with you. The intimate filmmaking approach transforms mundane daily routine into something unexpectedly moving.

Skip this one unless you have a specific investment in Telugu family drama. The runtime stretches thin material across nearly three hours without enough substance to justify the theatrical commitment.

ratings.plot: A filmocumentary about the founding fathers 'darkest hour' up to and including the Declaration of Independance. Narrated by Tom Selleck and available as a short run, in selected cinemas May 31st for the July 4th holiday.

verdict.reason: Skip this holiday-timed documentary unless you need patriotic background noise. Tom Selleck's narration cannot elevate what reads like a History Channel special stretched for theaters.
